stem: What is the smallest three-digit number with all of these properties: divisible by 6; contains the digit 3; has no digit 0; has all different digits; has exactly 1 odd digit?
trap: a solver who skips “has exactly 1 odd digit” stops at 132 — the planted decoy.
method: Apply the strong filters first (divisibility, no-zero, distinct), then check the SUBTLE last condition before you commit — don't stop at the first multiple you find.

stem: In how many ways can you choose 3 different numbers from 2 to 22 so that their sum is a multiple of 3?
why: C(21,3) = 1330 triples — impossible to list. Sort the numbers into remainder buckets mod 3 and count the bucket combinations.
method: Bucket all numbers by remainder mod 3; the valid triples are 'all-from-one-bucket' + the matching cross-bucket combinations.

stem: How many four-digit numbers have all of these properties: divisible by 12; has all different digits; has no digit 0; has exactly 2 odd digits; has a digit sum divisible by 9; contains the digit 8?
why: ~9000 four-digit candidates — you cannot list them. Count structurally: fix the digit-sum/parity buckets and use divisibility.
method: Don't hunt for examples — partition by the binding condition (digit sum / parity) and count each class.

stem: You write the 250 numbers 4, 8, 12, ... up to 1000. How many times do you write the digit '0'?
why: 250 terms up to 1000 — you can't write them out. Count the digit '0' one place-column at a time (units, tens, hundreds).
method: Go place-column by place-column: count how often the digit appears in the units, then tens, then hundreds across the whole list.

stem: How many four-digit numbers have all of these properties: divisible by 13; has all different digits; has no digit 0; has exactly 2 odd digits; has a digit sum divisible by 9; contains the digit 6?
why: ~9000 four-digit candidates — you cannot list them. Count structurally: fix the digit-sum/parity buckets and use divisibility.
method: Don't hunt for examples — partition by the binding condition (digit sum / parity) and count each class.
